nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and rubble bottoms of shallow, sheltered reefs and lagoons; typically secretive among coral rubble below living coral; depth range 0–20 m

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on live harpacticoid copepods, amphipods, small crustaceans, and tiny benthic invertebrates picked from sand and rubble throughout the day. Wild-caught specimens have generally been reported to refuse prepared and frozen foods; captive-bred individuals may gradually learn to accept frozen Mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, or vitamin-enriched live brine. A mature tank (typically 6–12 months old) with a thriving in-tank pod population, ideally supported by an attached refugium, is widely considered essential for long-term success.

avoidWith: other male Spotted or Green Mandarins
competitive feeders (dottybacks, damselfish, anthias, basslets, most gobies)

summary: A vividly patterned dragonet from the Indo-West Pacific with green and blue ringed spots on a pale body; generally considered expert-only due to its strict reliance on live copepod populations and need for a mature reef tank.
careNotes:
The Spotted Mandarin (Synchiropus picturatus), also widely sold as the Psychedelic Mandarin or Picturesque Dragonet, is closely related to the Green Mandarin (S. splendidus) and shares essentially the same demanding husbandry requirements. Its body pattern is distinct — a pale grayish-green to olive background marked with concentric blue-and-black ringed 'target' spots, rather than the swirling green-and-orange labyrinth of S. splendidus — but the diet, tank requirements, and behaviour overlap heavily.

Like other Callionymidae, it has been documented continuously grazing on small live prey (harpacticoid copepods, amphipods, polychaete worms, small crustaceans). Wild-caught specimens have generally been observed to refuse prepared or frozen foods; starvation has been widely reported as the leading cause of death in captivity. A mature tank with a self-sustaining pod population — typically 6–12 months old, ideally with an attached refugium acting as a continuous pod source — is generally considered essential. Captive-bred specimens, where available, tend to be substantially easier to feed.

Males are distinguishable by an elongated first dorsal fin spine. Only one male should be kept per display tank; males have been widely reported to fight aggressively, often fatally. A single male can be kept with one or more females, and mated pairs may spawn in captivity in a behavior similar to S. splendidus (ascending together at dusk to release pelagic eggs).

As a scaleless dragonet covered in a thick, foul-tasting mucus, the species is generally considered highly resistant to ich and other ectoparasites when feeding well — but is also highly sensitive to copper-based medications and should not be treated with them.

Cross-source notes — tank size: LiveAquaria cites 30 US gal (~114 L); other hobbyist sources cite 50–75 US gal (~190–284 L) as preferable for sustaining adequate pod populations. The conservative value of 200 L used here biases toward the larger end of the range because pod availability is the practical limiting factor for long-term survival, not territorial requirement. Temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22.2–25.6 °C; FishBase wild data gives 26.9–29 °C; Top Shelf Aquatics cites 23.9–26.7 °C. The averaged aquarium range of 24–27 °C is used.

Lifespan reports vary from ~5 years (commonly cited) to 10+ years in well-kept systems; the range 5–10 years is used.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is achievable with a mated male–female pair and has been documented in aquaria, occurring in the evening with both fish ascending together to release pelagic eggs and sperm. Larvae are extremely small and require rotifers followed by copepod nauplii as first foods. Rearing larvae to juvenile stage is considered advanced and has been accomplished by a small number of specialist breeders; commercial captive-bred S. picturatus juveniles are available but uncommon.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Dragonet collection page used as primary entry; species-specific product page at /products/spotted-mandarin confirmed care level (difficult), minimum tank (30 gal / ~114 L), adult size (~3 in / 7.6 cm), temperature (72–78 °F / 22.2–25.6 °C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), peaceful temperament, reef-safe yes, native range (Western Pacific), and trade names (Psychedelic Mandarinfish, Picture Dragonet).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified scientific name and authority (S. picturatus, Peters 1877), taxonomy (Callionymidae / Callionymiformes), maximum length 7.0 cm TL, wild temperature 26.9–29 °C, depth range 0–20 m, distribution (Philippines, eastern Indonesia, NW Australia), and habitat (sandy bottoms of shallow sheltered reefs, secretive in rubble below living coral). FishBase wild temperature range is higher than aquarium-source recommendations; values averaged to 24–27 °C for the recommended aquarium range.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics — Mandarin and Dragonet Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verified adult size (3–4 in / 7–10 cm), minimum tank (50–75 gal / ~190–284 L), water parameters (temperature 23.9–26.7 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.024–1.026), and lifespan (10+ years in well-kept systems). Tank-size recommendation notably larger than LiveAquaria's 30 gal; conservative-larger 200 L value chosen per husbandry guidance that pod availability is the limiting factor.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Mandarin Goby
additional.notes: Used for additional cross-check on Mandarinidae husbandry generally; noted that S. picturatus is treated as a color/morph variant by some hobbyist sources rather than a distinct species, but FishBase confirms it is a valid distinct species (Peters 1877). No species-specific numeric divergence from other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning has been reported with a mated pair; larval rearing requires rotifers then copepod nauplii and is considered advanced-level.
